content:
# Acts Chapter 3 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios from Quire 86 (PDF pages 227-228) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_003_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_003_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

### The Healing at the Beautiful Gate

1 Now Peter and John were going up to the temple at the hour of prayer, the ninth hour.

2 And a certain man, lame from his mother's womb, was being carried, whom they placed daily at the gate of the temple called Beautiful, to ask alms from those entering the temple.

3 He, seeing Peter and John about to enter the temple, asked to receive alms.

4 And Peter, gazing at him together with John, said, "Look at us."

5 And he fixed his attention on them, expecting to receive something from them.

6 But Peter said, "Silver and gold I do not possess, but what I have, this I give to you: in the name of Jesus Christ the Nazarene, rise up and walk."

7 And seizing him by the right hand, he raised him up; and immediately his feet and ankles were made firm.

8 And leaping up, he stood and began to walk, and he entered the temple with them, walking and leaping and praising God.

9 And all the people saw him walking and praising God,

10 and they recognized him, that this was the one who sat for alms at the Beautiful Gate of the temple; and they were filled with astonishment and amazement at what had happened to him.

11 And while he was holding fast to Peter and John, all the people ran together to them at the portico called Solomon's, utterly astonished.

### Peter's Sermon at Solomon's Portico

12 And when Peter saw this, he addressed the people, "Men of Israel, why do you marvel at this, or why do you gaze at us, as though by our own power or godliness we had made him walk?

13 The God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob, the God of our fathers, glorified his servant Jesus, whom you delivered up and denied in the presence of Pilate, when he had decided to release him.

14 But you denied the Holy and Righteous One, and asked for a murderer to be granted to you,

15 and you killed the Author of life, whom God raised from the dead, of which we are witnesses.

16 And upon the basis of faith in his name, his name has strengthened this man whom you see and know; and the faith that is through him has given him this complete soundness in the presence of you all.

### The Call to Repentance

17 "And now, brothers, I know that you acted in ignorance, just as your rulers also did.

18 But what God foretold through the mouth of all the prophets, that his Christ would suffer, he thus fulfilled.

19 Repent therefore and turn back, so that your sins may be blotted out,

20 that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord, and that he may send the Christ appointed for you — Jesus,

21 whom heaven must receive until the times of restoration of all things, of which God spoke through the mouth of his holy prophets from of old.

22 Moses indeed said, 'The Lord God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers. You shall listen to him in all things, whatever he speaks to you.

23 And it shall be that every soul that does not listen to that prophet shall be utterly destroyed from the people.'

24 And all the prophets also, from Samuel and those who followed after, as many as spoke, also proclaimed these days.

25 You are the sons of the prophets and of the covenant which God made with your fathers, saying to Abraham, 'And in your seed shall all the families of the earth be blessed.'

26 To you first, God, having raised up his servant, sent him to bless you, in turning each one of you from your wickedness."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**General agreement:** The three translators show strong alignment across Acts 3. The healing narrative (vv. 1-10), the crowd's reaction (v. 11), and Peter's sermon (vv. 12-26) are rendered consistently in substance. Most differences are matters of register: EMET preserving Greek morphology and word order, LOGOS rendering formal English equivalence, and PNEUMA aiming for contemporary accessibility. The Sinaiticus manuscript images confirm the standard text with no significant variant readings affecting translation choices.

**Manuscript observations:** The first image (page 227) shows the rightmost column beginning Acts 3:1, with the transition from Acts 2:47 clearly visible. The second image (page 228) covers approximately 3:9 through the end of the chapter and into Acts 4. The uncial text is legible and consistent with the standard Sinaiticus transcription. No significant corrections or scribal alterations are visible in the Acts 3 portion.

**Key decisions:**

1. **Verse 6 — "rise up and walk" vs. "walk" (ἔγειρε καὶ περιπάτει / περιπάτει):** EMET omits "rise up," rendering only "walk." LOGOS includes "rise up and walk." PNEUMA renders "get up and walk." The Sinaiticus reading at this point supports the inclusion of the command to rise. The fuller form (ἔγειρε καὶ περιπάτει) appears in multiple early witnesses. I follow LOGOS, including "rise up and walk" as the more complete reading. EMET's omission may reflect a textual variant or editorial choice to follow manuscripts that have only περιπάτει.

2. **Verse 7 — "made firm" vs. "strengthened" (ἐστερεώθησαν):** EMET: "were-made-firm." LOGOS: "were strengthened." PNEUMA: "strength surged into." The Greek στερεόω means "to make firm, solid, strong" — it denotes firmness and solidity rather than mere strengthening. The same root appears in verse 16 (στερέωμα, "firmness/soundness"). I adopt "were made firm" with EMET, preserving the Greek root connection between the physical healing (v. 7) and Peter's theological explanation (v. 16, where I render the cognate noun as "soundness"). LOGOS's "strengthened" is acceptable but loses this lexical link.

3. **Verse 10 — "astonishment and amazement" vs. "wonder and astonishment" (θάμβους καὶ ἐκστάσεως):** EMET: "astonishment and amazement." LOGOS: "wonder and astonishment." PNEUMA: "completely overwhelmed — stunned." The Greek θάμβος means "astonishment, amazement" and ἔκστασις means "displacement of mind, astonishment, ecstasy." I follow EMET's word order: "astonishment and amazement." Both EMET and LOGOS capture the double expression; PNEUMA collapses the hendiadys into a single intensified phrase.

4. **Verse 12 — "answered" vs. "responded" vs. "spoke" (ἀπεκρίνατο):** EMET: "answered." LOGOS: "responded." PNEUMA: "spoke." The Greek ἀποκρίνομαι can mean "to answer" or more broadly "to respond, to address." In this context Peter is not answering a question but responding to a situation. I render "addressed" as a middle path — more precise than "spoke" (PNEUMA) and better suited to the context than "answered" (EMET), while capturing the responsive quality of ἀπεκρίνατο.

5. **Verse 13 — "servant" vs. "servant" (παῖδα):** All three translations agree on "servant" for παῖς. This is significant: παῖς can mean "child" or "servant/attendant." In the context of Acts 3:13, 26 it echoes the Servant Songs of Isaiah (LXX uses παῖς for Hebrew עֶבֶד). The consensus "servant" correctly preserves this Isaianic connection.

6. **Verse 15 — "Author" vs. "originator" (ἀρχηγόν):** EMET: "originator of the life." LOGOS: "Author of life." PNEUMA: "Author of life." The Greek ἀρχηγός means "leader, founder, originator, author" — one who begins or pioneers something. "Author of life" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) is the most natural and widely recognized English rendering. EMET's "originator" is lexically precise but less elegant. I follow the LOGOS/PNEUMA consensus.

7. **Verse 16 — "complete soundness" vs. "perfect health" (ὁλοκληρίαν):** EMET: "complete-soundness." LOGOS: "perfect health." PNEUMA: "complete healing." The Greek ὁλοκληρία means "completeness, wholeness, soundness" — from ὅλος (whole) + κλῆρος (allotment/portion), literally "having all parts complete." I adopt "complete soundness" with EMET. This preserves the Greek sense of wholeness-of-parts better than "perfect health" (which sounds modern/clinical) or "complete healing" (which focuses on process rather than result). The word describes the man's restored state — every part made whole.

8. **Verse 19 — "blotted out" vs. "wiped out/clean" (ἐξαλειφθῆναι):** EMET: "being-wiped-out." LOGOS: "blotted out." PNEUMA: "wiped clean." The Greek ἐξαλείφω means "to wipe out, wipe away, obliterate" — used of wiping a writing surface clean. "Blotted out" (LOGOS) is the traditional English rendering and captures the metaphor of erasure from a record. I follow LOGOS.

9. **Verse 20 — Word order: "Christ appointed for you — Jesus" vs. "Christ Jesus":** EMET: "the [one] having-been-appointed-before for-you, Christ Jesus." LOGOS: "the Christ appointed for you — Jesus." PNEUMA: "the Messiah appointed for you — Jesus." The Greek reads τὸν προκεχειρισμένον ὑμῖν Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν. The name "Jesus" comes after "Christ" with the participial phrase intervening, creating a dramatic delay: God will send the one appointed for you, the Christ — Jesus. LOGOS and PNEUMA both capture this rhetorical structure with the dash. I follow LOGOS.

10. **Verse 21 — "from of old" vs. "from the age" (ἀπ' αἰῶνος):** EMET: "from [the] age." LOGOS: "from of old." PNEUMA: "long ago." The Greek ἀπ' αἰῶνος means "from the age," i.e., from ancient times. "From of old" (LOGOS) is the most faithful formal rendering. PNEUMA's "long ago" is clear but loses the solemnity. EMET's "from the age" is overliteral for English. I follow LOGOS.

11. **Verse 22 — "brothers" vs. "own people" (ἀδελφῶν):** EMET and LOGOS: "brothers." PNEUMA: "your own people." The Greek ἀδελφῶν means "brothers" (in context, fellow Israelites). PNEUMA's "your own people" is an interpretive expansion. While the meaning is correct, "brothers" preserves the language of the Deuteronomy 18:15 quotation. I follow EMET and LOGOS.

12. **Verse 23 — "utterly destroyed" vs. "completely cut off" (ἐξολεθρευθήσεται):** EMET and LOGOS: "utterly destroyed." PNEUMA: "completely cut off." The Greek ἐξολεθρεύω means "to utterly destroy, to root out." This is covenantal language from Deuteronomy — the penalty for rejecting the prophet like Moses is utter destruction from the covenant community. "Utterly destroyed" (EMET, LOGOS) preserves the severity of the Greek verb. PNEUMA's "cut off" softens the language. I follow EMET and LOGOS.

13. **Verse 25 — "sons" vs. "heirs" (υἱοί):** EMET and LOGOS: "sons." PNEUMA: "heirs." The Greek υἱοί means "sons." PNEUMA's "heirs" is an interpretive rendering — while sonship implies inheritance, the Greek word is unambiguously "sons." "Heirs" imports a Pauline theological concept (cf. Gal 3:29, 4:7) not present in Peter's diction here. I follow EMET and LOGOS with "sons."

14. **Verse 25 — "covenanted" vs. "made" (διέθετο):** EMET: "covenanted." LOGOS: "made." PNEUMA: "made." The Greek διατίθημι in the middle voice means "to arrange, to dispose, to make a covenant." EMET's "covenanted" is more precise and preserves the covenantal language, but "made" (with "covenant" as the object) is standard and clear English. I follow LOGOS and PNEUMA with "made" for readability.

15. **Verse 26 — "wickedness" vs. "wickednesses" (πονηριῶν):** EMET: "wickednesses" (plural, matching Greek). LOGOS: "wickedness" (English singular). PNEUMA: "wicked ways." The Greek πονηριῶν is genitive plural. English typically uses the singular "wickedness" as an abstract noun, and LOGOS's rendering is natural. PNEUMA's "wicked ways" is a dynamic expansion. I follow LOGOS.

---
## Disagreement Log

### Verse 6 — Inclusion of "rise up" (ἔγειρε)

**The variant:** Whether the command includes "rise up and walk" or simply "walk."

- **EMET** rendered only "walk" (omitting ἔγειρε).
- **LOGOS** rendered "rise up and walk" (including ἔγειρε καὶ περιπάτει).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "get up and walk" (including the command to rise).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "rise up and walk." The fuller reading is supported by major witnesses. EMET's omission may follow a shorter textual tradition, but the command to rise is well attested and thematically important — the man has never stood, so "rise up" marks the miraculous nature of the healing.

### Verse 7 — ἐστερεώθησαν: "made firm" or "strengthened"?

**The variant:** The verb στερεόω carries nuances of firmness vs. general strength.

- **EMET** rendered "were-made-firm."
- **LOGOS** rendered "were strengthened."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "strength surged into" (dynamic paraphrase).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET with "were made firm." The Greek στερεόω specifically denotes making solid or firm (cf. the cognate στερέωμα, "firmament," in the LXX of Gen 1:6). The word creates a lexical connection with ὁλοκληρία ("complete soundness") in verse 16. "Strengthened" (LOGOS) is acceptable but less precise. PNEUMA's dynamic rendering adds narrative color not present in the Greek.

### Verse 10 — θάμβους καὶ ἐκστάσεως: "astonishment and amazement" or "wonder and astonishment"?

**The variant:** Two near-synonyms for astonishment are rendered with slightly different English pairs.

- **EMET** rendered "astonishment and amazement."
- **LOGOS** rendered "wonder and astonishment."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "completely overwhelmed — stunned."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET with "astonishment and amazement." The Greek hendiadys θάμβος καὶ ἔκστασις pairs two intense emotional responses. "Astonishment and amazement" preserves both terms without collapsing them (PNEUMA) and maps more directly to the Greek word order.

### Verse 15 — ἀρχηγόν: "Author" or "originator"?

**The variant:** The title ἀρχηγός applied to Jesus can be rendered in several ways.

- **EMET** rendered "originator of the life."
- **LOGOS** rendered "Author of life."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "Author of life."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS and PNEUMA with "Author of life." The Greek ἀρχηγός means "leader, founder, pioneer, author" — one who initiates or originates. "Author" in English captures both the creative and authoritative dimensions of the word. EMET's "originator" is lexically precise but lacks the elevated register appropriate to a christological title. The definite article with "life" (τῆς ζωῆς) is rendered without the article in English for naturalness, following LOGOS and PNEUMA.

### Verse 16 — ὁλοκληρίαν: "complete soundness" or "perfect health"?

**The variant:** The rare noun ὁλοκληρία is rendered with different English equivalents.

- **EMET** rendered "complete-soundness."
- **LOGOS** rendered "perfect health."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "complete healing."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET with "complete soundness." The Greek ὁλοκληρία (from ὅλος + κλῆρος) denotes wholeness in every part — a state of completeness rather than the process of healing (PNEUMA) or a modern medical concept (LOGOS). This word appears only here in the New Testament, and its etymological force — "having every allotted part" — is best captured by "soundness," meaning the man's body is now fully sound.

### Verse 22 — ἀδελφῶν: "brothers" or "own people"?

**The variant:** The Mosaic prophecy quotation uses ἀδελφῶν, rendered differently.

- **EMET** rendered "brothers."
- **LOGOS** rendered "brothers."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "your own people."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET and LOGOS with "brothers." The Deuteronomy 18:15 quotation in the LXX uses ἀδελφῶν ("brothers"), referring to fellow Israelites. PNEUMA's "your own people" is an interpretive expansion that, while conveying the sense, departs from the specific language of the prophetic text being quoted. In a quotation, fidelity to the source wording is paramount.

### Verse 23 — ἐξολεθρευθήσεται: "utterly destroyed" or "completely cut off"?

**The variant:** The covenantal penalty verb is rendered with differing severity.

- **EMET** rendered "utterly-destroyed out of the people."
- **LOGOS** rendered "utterly destroyed from the people."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "completely cut off from the people."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET and LOGOS with "utterly destroyed from the people." The Greek ἐξολεθρεύω is a strong verb of total destruction, used in the LXX for the utter annihilation prescribed for covenant-breakers (cf. Deut 18:19, Lev 23:29). PNEUMA's "cut off" (which translates a different Hebrew/Greek term, כָּרַת / ἐξολεθρεύω being stronger) softens the covenantal gravity of the warning.

### Verse 25 — υἱοί: "sons" or "heirs"?

**The variant:** Peter's address to the crowd uses the kinship term υἱοί.

- **EMET** rendered "sons of the prophets."
- **LOGOS** rendered "sons of the prophets."
- **PNEUMA** rendered "heirs of the prophets."

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows EMET and LOGOS with "sons." The Greek υἱοί unambiguously means "sons." PNEUMA's "heirs" imports a theological interpretation — sonship does imply inheritance, but the Greek word is not κληρονόμοι (heirs). Peter's language here emphasizes relational continuity with the prophets and the covenant, not specifically the inheritance motif that Paul develops elsewhere (Gal 3-4).

### Verse 26 — "sent him to bless you" vs. "sent him blessing you"

**The variant:** The participial construction εὐλογοῦντα ὑμᾶς is rendered differently.

- **EMET** rendered "sent him blessing you" (participle of manner/attendant circumstance).
- **LOGOS** rendered "sent him to bless you" (purpose infinitive interpretation).
- **PNEUMA** rendered "sent him to you first — to bless you" (purpose, expanded).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Consensus follows LOGOS with "sent him to bless you." The Greek participle εὐλογοῦντα can express purpose or manner. In context, purpose is clearer in English: God sent his servant for the purpose of blessing. EMET's "sent him blessing you" is grammatically faithful to the participle but awkward in English. LOGOS achieves the right balance.

---

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
